9: Pater hemon ho en tois ouranois — Our Father who is in the heavens — the address combines intimacy (Pater = Father, probably translating Aramaic Abba) with transcendence (en tois ouranois = in the heavens, indicating divine majesty). hagiastheto to onoma sou — hallowed be your name — the passive imperative (hagiastheto) is a divine passive: may God himself act to hallow his name (sanctify it before the world). This is not a prayer that we will reverence the name but that God will act eschatologically to vindicate his own name (Ezekiel 36:23).
12: aphes hemin ta opheilemata hemon — forgive us our debts — opheilema (debt) is the commercial-legal term for what is owed; sin as moral debt to God is a Semitic conceptual framework (Aramaic hoba = debt = sin). The aorist aphes (forgive, once) vs. the perfect in aphekamen (we have forgiven, already done) — the community's forgiveness of others is presented as the completed precondition, not a parallel request.
24: mammonas (mammonas) — Mammon — an Aramaic loanword (mammon), wealth or possessions as a personified power; Jesus personifies wealth as a rival lord (kyrios). ou dynasthe theo douleuein kai mammona — you cannot serve God and Mammon — the impossibility is structural: a slave belongs entirely to one master; divided service is definitionally impossible in the slave-master relationship. The two masters are presented as mutually exclusive rivals for ultimate loyalty.
1: tēn dikaiosynēn hymōn mē poiein emprosthen tōn anthrōpōn — do not perform your righteousness before people — dikaiosynē here governs the three practices that follow (almsgiving, prayer, fasting); it is the broader category of covenant faithfulness, not righteousness as legal standing. Theathēnai autois — to be seen by them — aorist infinitive of purpose: the audience-seeking is the problem, not the practice itself. Misthon ouk echete para tō patri hymōn — you have no reward from your Father — the reward from God and the reward from human observers are mutually exclusive.
2: mē salpises emprosthen sou hōsper hoi hypokritai poiousin — do not sound a trumpet before you as the hypocrites do — hypokritai is from hypokrinomai, to play a theatrical role; in Hellenistic Greek, a theater actor. Jesus applies it to religious performance where the inner motive contradicts the outer act. Apechousin ton misthon autōn — they have received their reward in full — apechō is the technical term from papyrus receipts: "paid in full." The heavenly ledger shows zero balance; they have been paid entirely by human applause.
3: mē gnōtō hē aristera sou ti poiei hē dexia sou — do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing — gnōtō third-person aorist imperative: let it not know. The image targets the reflex of self-congratulation: even internal awareness of one's own generosity becomes a form of self-audience. The left hand (aristera) and right hand (dexia) are the same person's own faculties; the command points to unawareness of self as the ideal of pure giving.
4: hopōs ē sou hē eleēmosynē en tō kryptō — so that your giving may be in secret (kryptos, hidden) — the purpose clause governs the action: secrecy is the goal, not a byproduct. Ho patēr sou ho blepōn en tō kryptō apodōsei soi — your Father who sees in secret will reward you — blepōn present participle: ongoing active observation; apodōsei future: certain future payment. The Father's observation substitutes completely for human observation.
5: hoi hypokritai philousin — the hypocrites love — phileō (love by affection/inclination) rather than agapaō; they are drawn to public prayer by desire, not duty. Stantontes en tais synagōgais kai en tais gōniais tōn plateiōn proseuchesthai — standing in the synagogues and on the street corners to pray — stantontes is a perfect active participle (histamai): having taken their stand, remaining standing. The position is deliberate and held. Apechousin — paid in full — same receipt-formula as Matthew 6:2.
6: tameion — inner room, storeroom — a private interior room, often windowless, used for storage; the most private space in a household. Kleisas tēn thyran sou — having shut your door — kleisas aorist participle: the closed door is a precondition, not an afterthought. Proseuxai tō patri sou tō en tō kryptō — pray to your Father who is in secret — the Father inhabits the hidden space; he is present in the hiddenness itself. Ho blepōn en tō kryptō apodōsei soi — identical reward-formula to Matthew 6:4.
7: battalogēsēte — do not babble (battologeō) — a verb found almost nowhere in classical Greek, likely onomatopoeic: stuttering, repeating nonsense syllables. Hōsper hoi ethnikoi — like the Gentiles — pagan prayer often involved extensive repetition of divine names and epithets to compel divine attention. Dokousin gar hoti en tē polylogia autōn eisakousthēsontai — for they think that by their many words (polylogia, a word coined for the occasion) they will be heard — the theology of prayer-by-volume contrasted with address to a Father who already knows.
8: oiden ho patēr hymōn hōn chreian echete — your Father knows what you need — oiden is a perfect form used as a present: the Father's knowledge is complete and current. Pro tou hymas aitēsai auton — before you ask him — prayer is not informational; it is relational. Prayer does not supply God with data he lacks but is the mode of relationship in which the Father's foreknowledge becomes the context for the disciple's dependence.
10: elthatō hē basileia sou — let your kingdom come (elthátō aorist imperative of erchomai) — the petition is eschatological: the full arrival of the reign of God. Genēthētō to thelēma sou — let your will be done (genēthētō aorist passive imperative of ginomai): let it come to be. Hōs en ouranō kai epi gēs — as in heaven, so also on earth — the adverbial phrase qualifies how the will is to be done: the manner of heavenly obedience is the standard for earthly obedience.
11: ton arton hēmōn ton epiousion dos hēmin sēmeron — give us today our daily bread — epiousion (G1967) is one of the rarest words in the Greek New Testament, found almost nowhere outside this prayer and its Lukan parallel (Matthew 11:3). It may derive from epi + ousia (for existence: the bread necessary for existence) or epi + iōn (for the coming day: tomorrow's bread). Origen noted that the word did not exist in classical Greek, suggesting it was coined for the prayer. The MKT "daily" follows the most common rendering; "necessary for existence" or "for the coming day" are defensible alternatives.
13: mē eisenenkēs hēmas eis peirasmon — do not lead us into temptation/testing (peirasmos) — eisenenkēs aorist active subjunctive: a point-action. Peirasmos covers both testing (a trial through which faith is proved) and temptation (an inducement to sin); the petition covers both senses. Alla rhysai hēmas apo tou ponērou — but deliver us from the evil one — rhysai aorist imperative of rhyomai (to rescue from danger); apo tou ponērou likely personal (cf. Matthew 5:37). The doxology (hoti sou estin hē basileia) is well-attested in later manuscripts but absent from the earliest witnesses (Sinaiticus, Vaticanus).
14: ean gar aphēte tois anthrōpois ta paraptōmata autōn — for if you forgive people their trespasses — paraptōmata (G3900, from parapiptō, to fall beside/astray): trespasses, moral deviations — a different word from opheilēmata (debts) in Matthew 6:12. Aphēte aorist active subjunctive: a decisive act of forgiveness. Aphēsei kai hymin ho patēr hymōn ho ouranios — your heavenly Father will also forgive you — the correlation is conditional, not commercial; human forgiveness is evidence of a heart transformed by divine forgiveness, not its cause.
15: ean de mē aphēte tois anthrōpois — but if you do not forgive people — the negative counterpart to Matthew 6:14 using the same subjunctive construction. Oude ho patēr hymōn aphēsei ta paraptōmata hymōn — neither will your Father forgive your trespasses — the conditional symmetry means that withholding forgiveness reveals a heart that has not received forgiveness at depth (cf. Matthew 18:23-35, the unforgiving servant), not that human forgiveness earns divine forgiveness.
16: nēsteuēte mē ginesthe hōsper hoi hypokritai skythropoi — when you fast, do not become like the hypocrites, gloomy-faced — skythropoi (gloomy, sullen: skythros + ōps/face) appears in the LXX of Genesis 40:7 for the cup-bearer's distress. Aphanizousin gar ta prosōpa autōn — for they disfigure their faces (aphanizō, to make unseen/disappear) — they obscure their faces to make their piety visible; the irony: they make their faces invisible in order to be seen. Apechousin — paid in full — same formula as Matthew 6:2, 5.
17: su de nēsteuōn aleipsai sou tēn kephalēn kai to prosōpon sou nipsai — but when you fast, anoint your head and wash your face — aleipsai aorist middle imperative of aleiphō (to anoint with oil): anointing the head was a sign of festivity, not mourning (Psalms 23:5; Ruth 3:3). Nipsai aorist middle imperative of niptō (to wash). The commands describe normal daily grooming; Jesus instructs disciples to look normal, i.e., to conceal the fast from human observers entirely.
18: hopōs mē phanēs tois anthrōpois nēsteuōn — so that you may not appear to be fasting to people — phanēs aorist passive subjunctive of phainō: appear, be visible. The purpose of normal grooming is concealment of the practice. Ho patēr sou ho blepōn en tō kryptō apodōsei soi — identical reward-formula to 6:4 and Matthew 6:6; the three practices of piety (alms, prayer, fasting) receive the identical structural treatment.
19: mē thēsaurizete hymin thēsaurous epi tēs gēs — do not store up treasures for yourselves on earth (thēsaurizete present imperative: stop the ongoing practice) — hopou sēs kai brōsis aphanizei — where moth and corrosion destroys — sēs (moth, the insect that consumes fabric); brōsis (eating, corrosion — used for rust or vermin gnawing grain). Kai hopou kleptai dioryssousin — and where thieves dig through (dioryssō) — thieves in ancient Palestine literally dug through the mud-brick walls of homes rather than forcing doors.
20: thēsaurizete de hymin thēsaurous en ouranō — but store up for yourselves treasures in heaven — the positive counterpart to Matthew 6:19 with the same verb thēsaurizete (present imperative); the contrast is location, not activity. Hopou oute sēs oute brōsis aphanizei, kai hopou kleptai ou dioryssousin — the negative catalogue reverses Matthew 6:19 point by point. The treasure-in-heaven language draws on Jewish tradition (Tob Matthew 4:9; Pss. Sol. Matthew 9:5) where righteous deeds credited before God constitute heavenly wealth.
21: hopou gar estin ho thēsauros sou, ekei estai kai hē kardia sou — for where your treasure is, there your heart will be also — thēsauros and kardia are linked by the directional hopou/ekei (where/there). This reverses the expected psychology: one might expect "where your heart is, there you will put your treasure." Jesus says that what you invest in determines what you care about. The kardia is the center of loyalty and attention, not merely emotion.
22: ho lychnos tou sōmatos estin ho ophthalmos — the lamp of the body is the eye — the body is illuminated by a single opening. Ean oun ē ho ophthalmos sou haplous — if then your eye is haplous — haplous (G573) means single, undivided, and in Jewish idiom generous (in contrast to the "evil eye" of Matthew 6:23 which denotes stinginess); both senses are active here. Holon to sōma sou phōteinon estai — your whole body will be full of light — the single eye illuminates the entire body; the eye metaphor carries the treasure/heart teaching of Matthew 6:19-21 into the language of perception.
23: ean de ho ophthalmos sou ponēros ē — but if your eye is evil/diseased — ponēros (evil, and: diseased, not functioning properly). In Jewish idiom, the "evil eye" (ayin ra) denoted stinginess or greed; the "good eye" (ayin tob) denoted generosity. Both the functional (seeing clearly/poorly) and the moral (generous/stingy) dimensions are present. Ei oun to phōs to en soi skotos estin, to skotos poson — if then the light that is in you is darkness, how great is that darkness — the rhetorical question uses the impossible image (a darkness that functions as light) to measure the magnitude of the self-deception.
25: mē merimnate tē psychē hymōn ti phagēte ē ti piēte — do not be anxious (merimnaō, present imperative: stop the ongoing anxiety) about your life/soul, what you will eat or drink — psychē (G5590) here = life, the physical life sustained by food and drink. Mēde tō sōmati hymōn ti endysēsthe — nor for your body what you will put on. Ouchi hē psychē pleon estin tēs trophēs — is not life more than food? — a qal va-chomer (light-to-heavy) argument: if God provided the greater thing (life itself), will he not provide the lesser things that sustain it?
26: emblepate eis ta peteina tou ouranou — look carefully at the birds of the air (emblepō, to look into/at intently — stronger than blepō) — the birds are not examples of industriousness but of provision despite having no storage system: ou speirousin oude therizousin oude synagousin eis apothēkas — they neither sow nor reap nor gather into barns. Ho patēr hymōn ho ouranios trephei auta — your heavenly Father feeds them. Ouch hymeis mallon diapherete autōn — are you not worth much more than they — diapherō, to differ in value.
27: tis de ex hymōn merimnōn dynastai prostheinai epi tēn hēlikian autou pēchyn hena — which of you by worrying can add a single cubit to his span of life — hēlikia can mean height (stature) or lifespan; in context "span of life" makes better sense (adding a cubit to one's stature is trivial; adding time to one's lifespan is what worry seeks). Pēchys (a cubit, about 45 cm) applied to time is a small unit. The rhetorical question expects "no one."
28: ta krina tou agrou — the lilies/flowers of the field — krinon (G2918) likely refers to wildflowers broadly; archaeological work suggests the scarlet anemone or similar field flowers of Galilee, not necessarily the iris or water lily. Katamathete — observe carefully (katamanthanō, a stronger compound of manthanō, to learn) — learn by observation. Pōs auxanousin — how they grow — present indicative. Ou kopiōsi oude nēthousin — they do not labor nor do they spin — the activities of human textile production are absent from the flowers.
29: Solomōn en pasē tē doxē autou — Solomon in all his glory — doxa here = splendor, the visual manifestation of honor and wealth. Solomon's legendary wealth (1 Kgs 10) is the superlative of human magnificence. Ou periebleto hōs hen toutōn — was not clothed like one of these — periballō (to clothe, wrap around) aorist passive; Solomon as a passive recipient of clothing, compared unfavorably to what God clothes wild flowers with. The understatement makes the point more striking.
30: ton chorton tou agrou sēmeron onta kai aurion eis klibanon ballomenon — the grass of the field, which is here today and tomorrow thrown into the oven — chortos (grass, hay) encompasses lilies and common grass; klibanon is a clay pot or dome used as an oven, into which dried grass was thrown as fuel. Oligopistoi — O you of little faith — a compound word: oligos (little) + pistis (faith/trust); Matthew uses it as Jesus's characteristic address to disciples in distress (Matthew 8:26; Matthew 14:31; Matthew 16:8; Matthew 17:20). The diminutive is corrective, not contemptuous.
31: mē oun merimnēsēte legontes — do not therefore be anxious, saying — merimnēsēte aorist subjunctive: a prohibition against taking up anxiety as a posture. The three quoted worries (ti phagōmen, ti piōmen, ti peribalōmetha — what shall we eat? what shall we drink? what shall we wear?) are subjunctive deliberatives: the internal questions of anxious deliberation. Jesus quotes the exact questions the anxious person asks herself, making the prohibition specific and recognizable.
32: panta gar tauta ta ethnē epizētousin — for the Gentiles seek all these things (epizēteō, to seek after earnestly) — the ethnē here are not moral exemplars but the non-covenant people who have no framework for trusting a providential Father. Oiden gar ho patēr hymōn ho ouranios hoti chrēzete toutōn hapantōn — for your heavenly Father knows that you need all these things — the same foreknowledge as Matthew 6:8 (oiden perfect-as-present); the Father's prior knowledge removes the cognitive urgency for anxiety.
33: zēteite de prōton tēn basileian tou theou kai tēn dikaiosynēn autou — seek first his kingdom and his righteousness — zēteite present imperative, contrasting with the anxious seeking of Matthew 6:32; prōton sets the priority. Tēn dikaiosynēn autou — his righteousness — autou can be objective (the righteousness God requires) or subjective (the righteousness God himself displays); the MKT "his righteousness" preserves the ambiguity. Tauta panta prostethēsetai hymin — all these things will be added to you — prostithēmi future passive: provision comes as an addition to, not a substitute for, seeking the kingdom.
34: mē oun merimnēsēte eis tēn aurion — do not therefore be anxious about tomorrow — eis tēn aurion projects anxiety forward in time. Hē gar aurion merimnēsei heautēs — for tomorrow will be anxious for itself (heautēs reflexive genitive) — a personification of tomorrow as an agent carrying its own burden. Arketon tē hēmera hē kakia autēs — sufficient for the day is its own trouble — arketos (sufficient, from arkeō) + kakia (evil, trouble, hardship): the day's troubles are already enough; accumulating tomorrow's troubles before tomorrow arrives is unnecessary addition.
